title: Europe’s China Shock In Perspective
text:
Europe’s China Shock In Perspective

Cedric Gemehl

Gavekal Research | 18 Jun 2026

European policymakers have become obsessed with the inroads Chinese producers are making into Europe’s single market. Listen to the China hawks in Brussels today and you would be forgiven for thinking that Chinese firms are coming not only to steal your job, but also your spouse and probably eat your children too. Such hyperbole is perhaps justified given that European industry commissioner Stéphane Séjourné recently claimed that 29mn jobs in the EU are at risk from Chinese overproduction. For context, this amounts to 97.6% of the 29.7mn people employed in Europe’s manufacturing sector at the end of 2025.EU policymakers’ fixation on Chinese imports is a post-pandemic development, largely stemming from the rapid deterioration in the EU’s bilateral goods trade balance with China. In the five years to April 2026, the EU’s trade deficit with the Asian export powerhouse almost doubled, from €195bn to €378bn.
